text: The surgical team performs a simultaneous trans-anal transabdominal approach, indicating the use of both trans-anal and transabdominal access points for the procedure.
Trans-anal arthroscopy reveals a suspicious area of a fistula tract at the five o'clock position, while transabdominal inspection shows no evident injury to the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon closes the fistula transanally using non-absorbable double figure-of-eight sutures, ensuring the closure is secure.
The surgeon uses a mono-curved coagulation hook inserted transanally to perforate the anterior surface of the rectal stump.
The surgeon adopts a rendezvous approach, temporarily inserting a small silicone tube into the abdomen during the simultaneous trans-anal transabdominal procedure.
The surgeon replaces the mono-curved coagulation hook with straight grasping forceps, visible within the trans-anal surgical field.
The surgeon passes the small silicone tube through the rectal stump and out of the anus, completing the transanal maneuver.
The surgeon connects the spike of a circular stapler to the silicone tube and introduces the stapler into the anus for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon removes the silicone tube and connects the spike of the circular stapler to the anvil, preparing for the next step in the colorectal procedure.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis using a circular stapler, joining the rectal and colonic ends with visible precision.
The visual inspection reveals a repaired fistula located inferior to the anastomosis, with no visible defects in the anastomosis itself.
The surgeon uses contrast-enhanced ICG perfusion to assess the perfusion of the anastomosis site, ensuring adequate blood flow before proceeding to create a protective ileostomy.
The anastomosis is visually located approximately five centimeters above the pectineal line within the colorectal region.
The surgeon locates and removes the previous intraluminal sutures from the colorectal anastomosis site.
The surgeon cleans the defect in the anastomosis using a coagulating hook, removing debris and preparing the area for further intervention.
The surgeon introduces sutures into the D-port and performs a new repair on the anastomosis site, located five centimeters from the pectineal line.
